blocks.text: James Skinner, General Manager, {{fact:f:E-2005-001.client}}
Re: Roadway Widening Study, Professional Engineering Services
Dear Mr. Skinner:

This letter sets out the terms on which Brackenridge Civil Group Inc will prepare a roadway widening study for {{fact:f:E-2005-001.client}}. It follows our meeting at your office and the plat and correspondence your staff furnished afterward. When you have signed and returned it, this letter is the agreement between us, and it supersedes what either of us said before it.
Scope of Services

The study addresses the widening of the roadway serving your property from its existing two-lane rural section to the section the county public works department will require as a condition of development approval. Our services are limited to preliminary engineering and to the report that documents it. Final construction drawings, permit applications, right-of-way plats, and services during construction are not included in this engagement and would be the subject of a separate agreement. We will perform the following:

items: Field survey of the existing roadway, ditches, drives, and utility appurtenances within the study limits, tied to the county's horizontal and vertical control.

Turning-movement and volume counts at the intersections within the study limits, taken on ordinary weekdays while school is in session, and a capacity analysis of each intersection under existing and projected conditions.
Development of a typical section and of a horizontal and vertical alignment sufficient to establish the limits of grading and the right-of-way required.
Preliminary drainage analysis of the corridor, including the existing cross drains and the conveyance and detention the widened section will require.
Identification of utility conflicts from record information furnished by the utility owners, and a written summary of the relocations the alignment appears to force.
An opinion of probable construction cost at the preliminary engineering level, and a written report presenting our findings, our recommended section, and the basis for it.

blocks.text: Our Approach

We will begin with the record information rather than with the field: your plat and boundary survey, the county's as-built drawings for the existing roadway and its cross drains, and the utility records. What that assembly leaves unanswered is what the survey is for, and we would rather spend the survey where the answers are missing than run a corridor twice. The counts follow the survey. The alignment work and the drainage analysis proceed together, because on a corridor of this kind the ditch grade and the profile are one question and not two, and a recommendation that separates them is a recommendation that gets sent back.

The report will carry one recommended section and the reasoning that produced it. It will note the alternatives we considered and why we set them aside. We do not present a menu and ask the client to engineer, and we do not qualify a recommendation so heavily that it commits us to nothing.
Project Team

Linda George, Project Engineer, will manage the engagement and will be your day-to-day contact. She is responsible for the drainage analysis and for the report, and she will attend the progress meetings at the Evans-Lee office and any meeting the county calls on this project. Jeffrey Perez, Design Technician, will prepare the base mapping, the exhibits, the typical section, and the quantity takeoffs that support the cost opinion. I will review the study and seal it before it is issued, as I do with everything that leaves this office. Where a question needs an answer from us, put it to Linda George; where it needs a decision from Evans-Lee, we will contact you directly and put the request in writing.
Fee and Schedule

Our fee for the services described above is a lump sum of {{fact:f:E-2005-001.fee}}, which includes the survey, the counts, our travel within the county, and reproduction of the report in the quantity you have requested. Work will commence on {{fact:f:E-2005-001.start}}, subject to your written authorization and to our receipt of the record information identified above. We estimate the study will be complete approximately four months thereafter. That estimate assumes the county and the utility owners answer our requests for record drawings within the ordinary time. If they do not, we will contact you when we see the delay and not after it has cost you the schedule.
Fees and Payment

We will invoice monthly for the portion of the work completed in the preceding month. Invoices are payable net thirty days from the date of the invoice. Sums outstanding beyond sixty days bear interest at the maximum rate permitted by law. Services outside the scope described above will not be performed without your written authorization, and when authorized they will be invoiced at our standard hourly rates then in effect.
Term and Termination

This agreement takes effect on the date of your signature below and continues until the study has been delivered and our final invoice paid. Either party may terminate on fourteen days written notice to the other. Upon termination you will pay us for services performed and expenses incurred through the effective date, and we will deliver our work product in the condition it then exists. We may suspend the work on written notice if an invoice remains unpaid beyond sixty days, and we are not responsible for the schedule consequences of a suspension so caused.
Confidentiality

We will hold the information you furnish in confidence and will not disclose it to a third party except as required to perform the study, and except as required by law or by an agency having jurisdiction over the project. You should understand that our submittals to the county on your behalf become public record when they are filed, and we cannot make them otherwise. We ask the same treatment of our report and drawings, which are instruments of service prepared for this project and are not to be used on another without our written consent.
Limitation of Liability

Our liability to {{fact:f:E-2005-001.client}} arising out of this agreement, whether in contract or in tort, shall not exceed the total fee paid to us under it. We do not warrant record information furnished by others, including the utility records and the county's as-built drawings, and where such information proves incorrect the consequences of the error are not ours to bear. The opinion of probable construction cost is an opinion prepared by an engineer familiar with the local market. It is not a bid, and it is not a guarantee that the work can be constructed for that figure.
Governing Law

This agreement is governed by the laws of the state in which the project is located. Any dispute the parties cannot resolve between themselves will be submitted to mediation before either commences litigation, and the cost of the mediator will be borne equally.

We are pleased to have the opportunity to perform this work, and we appreciate your confidence in the firm. If the foregoing is acceptable, please sign both copies where indicated, return one to this office, and retain the other for your file. If any part of it is not acceptable, telephone me and we will discuss it before you sign.
Very truly yours,
To: Roadway widening study team
From: Jeffrey Perez, Design Technician
Re: Kickoff, Roadway Widening Study for {{fact:f:E-2005-001.client}}

This memorandum records the assignments and the sequence of work agreed at the kickoff meeting, so that the project file reflects them. Work commences {{fact:f:E-2005-001.start}}. Linda George is project manager and the client contact runs through her. The objective of the study is a defensible recommended section for the corridor, with an alignment good enough to fix the grading limits and the right-of-way, a drainage analysis that stands up at the county counter, and a cost opinion the client can budget against. It is a preliminary engineering study. It is not construction drawings, and nothing in this file should start to look like construction drawings.

James Skinner, General Manager at Evans-Lee, is the client contact. He has the plat, the boundary survey, and the correspondence with the county, and Linda George will contact him for them. Nobody else telephones Evans-Lee about this project. A question that needs an answer from Evans-Lee goes to Linda George and she asks it. This is not ceremony. The client should hear one voice from this firm, and a call from three of us produces three answers and no record of any of them.
Workstreams and assignments:

items: 1. Record information. Linda George requests the county as-builts for the roadway and the cross drains, and the utility records from each owner. Log every request in the project folder with the date it went out. We have been made to wait for record drawings before, and the log is what proves who is waiting on whom.

2. Field survey and base mapping. Survey ties to county control. I build the base map from the survey and nothing else. If the survey and the as-builts disagree, the survey governs and the disagreement gets noted, not smoothed over.

3. Counts and capacity. Turning-movement and volume counts at the study intersections, ordinary weekdays, school in session. Counts taken while school is out are not the corridor and we will not use them.
4. Alignment and typical section. Linda George sets the horizontal and vertical alignment. I draft the typical section and the exhibits from it. The section changes only when she changes it.
5. Drainage. Linda George's work. I furnish the ditch cross sections and the culvert inventory from the survey.

6. Quantities and cost opinion. Mine. Takeoffs come off the alignment and the section as issued, not off a draft I happened to have open. I check them once forward and once backward, and I would rather hold a number a day than issue one that is wrong.
7. Report. Linda George writes it. I supply the exhibits and the quantity tables in the form she asks for.

blocks.text: Next steps, in order:

items: Requests for record information go out this week. Nothing else waits on them, but everything else is worth less until they arrive.
Survey crew scheduled once the study limits are fixed on a marked-up aerial. Linda George marks it up; I will not guess at the limits.
Counts ordered after the limits are set, so we count the intersections that are in the study and not the ones that are near it.

Project folder lettered and opened on the file share before any of the above. Correspondence, requests, and the count sheets go in it the day they exist. A document that lives on somebody's desk is not in the file.
Progress meeting with Evans-Lee once the base map and the counts are in hand. Linda George runs it.

blocks.text: One point on the file, and then I am done. Everything that goes to the client or the county on this project is reviewed and sealed before it leaves. That includes the exhibits. If you are not certain a sheet is ready to be seen outside this office, it is not ready, and Linda George would rather be asked than told afterward.
Progress Meeting Minutes: Roadway Widening Study

Progress meeting held at the {{fact:f:E-2005-001.client}} office to review the base mapping, the counts, the working alignment, and the drainage approach. These minutes record what was decided. Where a matter was discussed and not decided, the minutes say so.
Attendees:

items: Linda George, Project Engineer, Brackenridge Civil Group Inc
Jeffrey Perez, Design Technician, Brackenridge Civil Group Inc
James Skinner, General Manager, Evans-Lee

blocks.text: Status. The study is at its midpoint and the work is where it should be. The survey is complete and the base map is built from it. The counts were taken on ordinary weekdays with school in session and the capacity analysis is written. The working alignment is drawn and the drainage analysis is under way against it. Nothing has been issued to the county and nothing will be until the report is sealed.

Alignment. Jeffrey Perez walked the working alignment and the typical section against the base map. The section holds through the corridor except at the north end, where the existing ditch line and the property line converge and the widened section will not fit between them without either a retaining structure or additional right-of-way. I recommended the right-of-way, on the ground that a wall here costs more to build than the strip costs to acquire and costs again every year it is maintained. James Skinner asked whether the alignment could be shifted west instead. It can be shifted, and the shift moves the problem into the drive of the parcel opposite rather than solving it. He asked for the shifted case to be drawn so he can see the taking he would be trading. It will be drawn as an exhibit and it will not become the recommendation.

Drainage. The existing cross drain at the low point is undersized for the widened section, and it is undersized for the section that is there now. That is not a finding the county will let us leave alone once the study puts it on paper. The replacement structure is in the cost opinion. Detention is the open question: the widening adds impervious area, and whether the county requires detention for a roadway improvement of this scale depends on how the public works department reads its own ordinance. I have seen it read both ways in this county. We will carry the detention in the cost opinion as a line item and note the assumption, rather than leave the client to discover the requirement at permitting.

Utilities. Two conflicts from the record information, both at the north end and both with the overhead line. The utility owner has not answered our second request for records. Jeffrey Perez has the requests logged with the dates they went out. James Skinner said he would make a call to the utility, and I asked him to make it after our third written request goes out rather than in place of it, so that the record shows we asked properly.

Discussed and not decided. Whether the study should carry a construction phasing recommendation. James Skinner would like one; it is outside the scope of this engagement. He will consider whether he wants it enough to authorize it, and if he does the request comes in writing and the fee is discussed before any of it is drawn.
Action items:

rows: Draw the shifted alignment as a comparison exhibit, with the taking on each parcel shown
Complete the cross drain sizing and the replacement structure detail
Third written request to the utility owner for overhead line records
Telephone the utility owner after the third request is sent
Confirm the detention assumption with the county public works department
Advise whether phasing is to be added to the scope, in writing

blocks.text: Status Report: Roadway Widening Study
Prepared by Jeffrey Perez, Design Technician, for {{fact:f:E-2005-001.client}}. This report covers the period since the last report and states the position of the study at its midpoint.
Status

The study is on schedule. The survey is complete, the base map is built, the counts are taken and analyzed, and the working alignment and typical section are drawn. The drainage analysis is under way against that alignment. Half the work is behind us and the half in front of us is the half that produces the report, which is the ordinary shape of a study of this kind and not a sign that the back end is thin. One item is not on schedule and it is not ours: the utility owner has not answered our requests for records of the overhead line at the north end of the corridor. That is discussed under risks below.
Accomplishments this period

items: Field survey completed through the study limits and tied to county control. The base map is built from the survey. Where the survey and the county as-built drawings disagree, and they do at two locations, the survey governs and the disagreement is noted in the file rather than reconciled away.
Turning-movement and volume counts taken at the study intersections on ordinary weekdays with school in session. Capacity analysis completed for existing and projected conditions.
Working horizontal and vertical alignment drawn, and the typical section developed against it. Grading limits are established through the corridor except at the north end.
Culvert inventory and ditch cross sections extracted from the survey and furnished to Linda George for the drainage analysis.
Preliminary quantity takeoffs started against the working alignment. Takeoffs are checked once forward and once backward before any quantity enters the cost opinion.
Progress meeting held at your office to review the alignment, the drainage approach, and the utility conflicts.

blocks.text: Upcoming work

items: Comparison exhibit showing the shifted alignment at the north end against the recommended alignment, with the taking on each parcel shown, so the trade is visible rather than argued.
Cross drain sizing at the low point and the replacement structure detail. Linda George's work.
Utility conflict summary, once the overhead line records are in hand.
Completion of the quantity takeoffs and the opinion of probable construction cost at the preliminary engineering level.

Draft report, written by Linda George, with the exhibits and quantity tables supplied by this office in the form she requires. The draft goes to Evans-Lee for factual review before it is sealed and issued.

blocks.text: Risks

The utility records are the risk that can move the schedule. Two written requests have gone out and neither has been answered. The requests and their dates are logged in the project file. A third goes out this week. If the records do not arrive, the study can still be completed, but the utility conflict summary would have to be qualified as based on field observation rather than on record information, and a qualified summary is worth less to Evans-Lee at permitting than an unqualified one. We would rather say this now than explain it in the report.

The second risk is the detention question. The widening adds impervious area, and whether the county requires detention for a roadway improvement of this scale depends on how the public works department reads its ordinance. It has been read both ways in this county. We are carrying detention in the cost opinion as a line item with the assumption noted, and Linda George will confirm the reading with the county before the draft report. If the requirement is confirmed, the number is already in the budget the client is looking at. If it is not, the line comes out. Either way it will not be a surprise at permitting.

The third is the north end right-of-way. The recommendation will call for the strip rather than a retaining structure, on the ground that a wall costs more to build than the strip costs to acquire and costs again every year somebody has to maintain it. Acquisition is not within our scope and it is not within our control, and the schedule for the study does not depend on it. The schedule for the improvement will. The comparison exhibit is being drawn so that the trade between the strip and the shifted alignment can be seen rather than argued, and so that whoever makes that decision makes it with the takings in front of him.
Budget

The lump sum fee of {{fact:f:E-2005-001.fee}} is unchanged, and we do not expect it to change. Approximately half of it is earned as of this report, which tracks the work completed. No services outside the scope have been performed and none have been authorized. The phasing recommendation raised at the progress meeting remains outside the scope; if it is authorized in writing, the fee for it is discussed before any of it is drawn.
